concerns.text: The plan assumes 'gpt2-medium' (355M params) can be fine-tuned on a GitHub Actions free-tier runner (2 CPU, ~7 GB RAM) with 4-bit quantization. While inference might fit, fine-tuning (even LoRA/QLoRA) on CPU with 355M parameters typically exceeds 6-7 GB RAM due to optimizer states and activation buffers, even with aggressive quantization. The plan cites 'bitsandbytes (CPU-compatible version)' which is often unstable or non-existent for training on CPU; standard practice for CPU training of this scale requires much smaller models (e.g., DistilGPT2) or offloading to disk (extremely slow). This dataset/model size mismatch with the compute constraint makes the experiment infeasible as written.

The plan estimates 90 training runs (3 datasets * 5 seeds * 2 models * 3 epochs) to complete in 5 hours on a 2-CPU runner. Even with a tiny model, training 355M parameters on CPU is orders of magnitude slower than GPU. A single epoch of fine-tuning GPT-2 Medium on a small dataset on CPU can take hours. The projected runtime is physically impossible on the specified hardware, rendering the dataset/model choice inappropriate for the compute constraints.

The plan selects LAMBADA and Story Cloze as 'episodic memory' benchmarks. While they test long-context retention, they are primarily next-token prediction or multiple-choice tasks, not structured episodic recall tasks like bAbI. The 'episodic chunk' mapping for LAMBADA (predicting the last word) is tenuous compared to bAbI's explicit 'fact -> question' structure. The plan fails to justify why these specific datasets are appropriate for testing a 'spatial slot' assignment mechanism, which requires distinct, addressable facts. LAMBADA's continuous narrative may not map well to discrete 64-slot grids without significant, unverified preprocessing assumptions.
